questions.prompt: What is the largest source of freshwater on Earth?
questions.quip_correct: About 69% of Earth's freshwater is locked in glaciers and ice caps.
questions.quip_wrong: Glaciers and ice caps hold ~69% of all freshwater — far more than lakes or aquifers.

questions.prompt: What term describes an underground layer of rock or sediment that holds and transmits groundwater?
questions.quip_correct: Aquifers supply roughly half the world's drinking water.
questions.quip_wrong: An aquifer is the underground formation — a watershed is the surface drainage area.

questions.prompt: What chemical is most commonly added to municipal drinking water to kill pathogens?
questions.quip_correct: Chlorination has been used since the early 1900s and remains the global standard.
questions.quip_wrong: Chlorine is the primary disinfectant — fluoride is added separately for dental health.

questions.prompt: Which water treatment process uses a semi-permeable membrane to remove dissolved salts from seawater?
questions.quip_correct: RO pushes water through membranes at high pressure, leaving salts behind.
questions.quip_wrong: Reverse osmosis (RO) is the key desalination technology — the others are conventional treatment steps.

questions.prompt: Which sector accounts for the largest share of global freshwater withdrawals?
questions.quip_correct: Agriculture uses roughly 70% of all freshwater withdrawn globally.
questions.quip_wrong: Agriculture accounts for ~70% of global freshwater use — mostly for irrigation.

questions.prompt: Drip irrigation conserves water compared to flood irrigation primarily by doing what?
choices: Delivering water directly to plant root zones, minimizing evaporation and runoff
Cooling the air around crops to reduce evaporation
Recycling wastewater before it reaches the field
Using underground pipes to pressurize soil moisture
questions.quip_correct: Drip systems can cut agricultural water use by 30–50% versus flood irrigation.
questions.quip_wrong: The key advantage is targeted delivery to roots — cutting evaporation and surface runoff.

questions.prompt: The 'prior appropriation' doctrine of water law is best described as:
choices: Water belongs to the land it flows through
First in time, first in right — senior users take priority in dry years
Water is shared equally among all users in a basin
Federal government owns all navigable waterways
questions.quip_correct: Prior appropriation dominates water law in the western United States.
questions.quip_wrong: 'First in time, first in right' — senior rights holders get water before junior holders in a shortage.

questions.prompt: The UN Sustainable Development Goal 6 (SDG 6) targets universal access to what by 2030?
choices: Desalination plants in every coastal nation
A 50% reduction in industrial water use
Safe drinking water and sanitation for all people
questions.quip_correct: SDG 6 covers clean water AND sanitation — about 2 billion people still lack safe drinking water.
questions.quip_wrong: SDG 6 targets clean water and sanitation for all — a foundational human development goal.

questions.prompt: What is the primary purpose of a dam's spillway?
choices: Safely release excess water to prevent dam overtopping
Filter sediment before water enters the reservoir
questions.quip_correct: Spillways are the dam's safety valve — critical during high-inflow flood events.
questions.quip_wrong: The spillway safely diverts floodwater — overtopping can erode and fail even concrete dams.

questions.prompt: What does the term 'non-revenue water' (NRW) refer to in urban water systems?
choices: Treated water that is lost or unaccounted for before reaching paying customers
Stormwater that bypasses the treatment plant
Water traded without a financial contract
questions.quip_correct: Global NRW losses average ~30% — representing billions of dollars of wasted treated water.
questions.quip_wrong: NRW is treated water lost to leaks, theft, or meter errors — a major efficiency challenge worldwide.
